original_thesis: Understanding complexity through structured learning depth. Bloom's taxonomy structures HOW DEEPLY you understand; Feynman technique tests whether you really do; chaos theory and butterfly effect reveal what makes systems unpredictable; risk-vs-uncertainty distinguishes the knowable from the unknowable; systems thinking provides the frame.
corrected_thesis: The 'know what you don't know' stack. Bloom's taxonomy maps depth of understanding; dialectical reasoning tests ideas against their opposites; step-back creates distance from the immediate problem; risk-vs-uncertainty distinguishes quantifiable risk from genuine unknowns; true-uncertainty-navigation provides methods for operating under deep uncertainty; systems thinking reveals the interconnections. This stack builds the meta-awareness needed to reason about problems you can't fully model.
what_this_stack_defeats: False confidence in complex domains, confusing risk with uncertainty, shallow understanding masquerading as expertise
notes: Original thesis mentioned chaos-theory, butterfly-effect, Feynman-technique — none are members. Actual members form a coherent 'epistemic depth' stack centered on knowing the limits of your own understanding.
